Call the agency first for instructions before doing anything

hha.q: A client with diabetes is sweating, shaking, confused, and says they feel dizzy. Blood glucose readings are unavailable. The HHA should suspect:
choices: Stroke — call 911 immediately without doing anything else
Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) — give 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrates if the client is conscious
Hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) — withhold all food and call the nurse
A panic attack — have the client breathe into a paper bag

hha.q: An HHA finds a client on the floor. The client is awake and says their hip is very painful. The HHA should:
choices: Help the client up slowly and into a chair to assess the damage
Leave the client on the floor, keep them still and warm, and call for help immediately
Have the client try walking to see if the hip can bear weight
Apply ice and wait 30 minutes before calling anyone

hha.q: When completing a daily log for a client, the HHA should document:
choices: Only problems and emergencies — routine care doesn't need documentation
All care provided, client's condition, intake and output, and any observations
A brief note confirming the visit happened and nothing unusual occurred
Only what the supervising nurse asks them to record

hha.q: An HHA makes an error in the daily documentation — they wrote the wrong time for a medication observation. The correct action is to:
choices: Use white-out to cover the error and write the correct information over it
Black out the error completely so it cannot be read
Draw a single line through the error, write the correct information, and initial it
Tear out the page and start over on a fresh sheet

hha.q: An HHA is helping a client use a walker. The client should carry the walker by:
choices: Picking it up completely with each step to move forward
Sliding it along the floor to maintain continuous contact
Moving the walker forward first, then stepping into it — one side at a time
Letting the client choose whichever method they prefer

hha.q: After completing morning care, the HHA leaves a client sitting in a chair. Before leaving the room, the HHA should ensure:
choices: The TV is on so the client is entertained
The client has their phone or call device within reach and is positioned safely
The curtains are open for natural light
The client has a magazine to read

hha.q: A client with dementia becomes upset and repeatedly asks to go home, despite being in their own home. The BEST response by the HHA is:
choices: 'You ARE home — stop saying that.'
Acknowledge the feeling and redirect gently: 'You seem worried — let's have some tea together.'
Show the client their family photos to prove they are home
Tell the client they cannot leave until the HHA's shift is over

hha.q: A client tells the HHA, 'I've been thinking about ending my life. I just don't see the point anymore.' The HHA should:
choices: Tell the client that things will get better and change the subject
Promise not to tell anyone so the client continues to feel safe sharing
Take the statement seriously, stay with the client, and report immediately to the supervising nurse
Suggest the client call their family when they feel ready

hha.q: An HHA should avoid accepting gifts or money from clients because:
choices: It creates a conflict of interest and may violate agency policy and professional ethics
Gifts are only a problem if they are worth more than $50
It is acceptable for small gifts like food or flowers
Only the supervising nurse is allowed to accept gifts

hha.q: An HHA who must be absent for a scheduled visit should:
choices: Ask a neighbor to check on the client
Skip the visit and make up the time on a later visit
Notify the agency as soon as possible so coverage can be arranged
Ask a friend or family member to cover the visit

hha.q: An HHA has been caring for a terminally ill client for six months and has become emotionally attached. The client dies. The MOST appropriate way for the HHA to respond professionally is:
choices: Avoid all contact with the family after the death — it is no longer the HHA's role
Attend the funeral only if invited by the family
Acknowledge their own grief, seek support from their agency, and allow themselves to grieve appropriately
Move on immediately — professional caregivers are trained not to feel loss

hha.q: What does 'HHA' stand for?
choices: Hospital Health Assistant
Home Health Aide
Healthcare Hygiene Associate
High-risk Health Agency
hha.exp: HHA = Home Health Aide. An HHA provides personal care and basic health services to clients in their own homes under supervision of a nurse or therapist.

hha.q: What does 'scope of practice' mean for an HHA?
choices: The size of the home they can work in
The specific tasks an HHA is legally allowed to perform
The geographic area they can work
The number of clients they can have
hha.exp: Scope of practice = the legal boundaries of what you are trained and licensed to do. HHAs provide personal care but do NOT administer medications, perform sterile procedures, or diagnose.

hha.q: Who creates and modifies the home care plan for an HHA's client?
choices: The HHA, based on daily observations
The client's family
The supervising nurse or therapist
The insurance company
hha.exp: The care plan is created by the supervising nurse or therapist. The HHA follows it exactly and reports any needed changes — never modifying it independently.

hha.q: What should an HHA do FIRST upon arriving at a new client's home?
choices: Begin cleaning immediately
Orient themselves to the emergency exits and phone
Ask the client what they want for lunch
Check the client's refrigerator for expired food
hha.exp: Safety first: know how to exit and call for help before providing care. Locate emergency exits, identify the nearest phone or confirm cell service.

hha.q: An HHA is changing a client's wound dressing at home. This task is:
choices: Within the HHA's scope of practice with proper training
Outside the HHA's scope — sterile wound care requires a nurse
Acceptable if the client or family requests it
Fine if the wound is small
hha.exp: Sterile wound care and dressing changes require a nurse. HHAs do not perform sterile procedures. The HHA should notify the supervising nurse.

hha.q: An HHA enters the client's kitchen and smells gas. The FIRST action is:
choices: Turn on the stove to check if it is the source
Open all windows and doors, and call 911 from outside
Light a match to see if there is a leak
Turn on the kitchen fan
hha.exp: Gas leaks are explosive. Do NOT create any spark (switches, matches, fans). Exit immediately, leave doors open, call 911 from outside.

hha.q: A client on a low-sodium diet asks the HHA to add extra salt to their food. The HHA should:
choices: Add the salt — the client has the right to eat what they want
Follow the dietary order and explain why salt is restricted for the client's health
Add a small amount so the client is happy
Call the doctor for permission before cooking
hha.exp: The HHA follows the care plan dietary orders. Explain the restriction kindly, use salt alternatives if approved, and report the request to the nurse.

hha.q: An HHA notices the client appears more confused than usual and is not eating. The BEST action is:
choices: Note it and report at the next scheduled home visit
Call the supervising nurse to report the change in condition
Try different foods until the client eats
Wait to see if it improves by tomorrow
hha.exp: A change in condition (new confusion, not eating) is a reportable event — do not wait. The supervising nurse must be notified promptly.

hha.q: A client refuses to take a bath. The HHA should:
choices: Force the bath since hygiene is part of the care plan
Respect the refusal, try to understand the reason, offer alternatives (partial bath, etc.), and document and report
Give up and never attempt personal care again
Tell the family to force the client to bathe
hha.exp: Clients have the right to refuse care. An HHA never forces care. Try alternatives, respect the right, document, and report to the nurse.

hha.q: A client's neighbor calls asking for information about the client's health condition. The HHA should:
choices: Share basic information since the neighbor is being friendly
Refuse to share any client information — HIPAA protects client privacy
Ask the client if it is okay to share
Refer the neighbor to the client directly and share nothing
hha.exp: HIPAA applies in the home. The HHA does not share client health information with anyone — neighbors, distant relatives, or friends — without proper authorization.

hha.q: An HHA's client tells her: 'I don't want to live anymore. I feel like a burden to everyone.' The BEST response is:
choices: 'Don't say that! Things will get better.'
'I understand you're feeling very down. I want to make sure you're safe — I'm going to call your nurse right now.'
'Let me distract you — let's watch TV together.'
'You should talk to your family about this, not me.'
hha.exp: Statements about not wanting to live must be taken seriously. Do not dismiss, deflect, or delay — report to the supervising nurse immediately. This is a potential safety crisis.

hha.q: An HHA discovers the client has chickenpox and the HHA has never had chickenpox or been vaccinated. The HHA should:
choices: Continue providing care while wearing a regular surgical mask
Contact the supervising nurse or agency before continuing care — possible airborne exposure risk
Wear gloves and a gown and continue normally
Refuse care without notification
hha.exp: Chickenpox requires airborne precautions. An unvaccinated/non-immune HHA faces a real infection risk. The supervising nurse must determine the appropriate action.

hha.q: The client's daughter asks the HHA to give her mother an extra blood pressure pill 'just this once' because the mother forgot her morning dose. The HHA should:
choices: Give the pill — it's just one dose and the daughter is family
Decline and call the supervising nurse to report the missed dose and request guidance
Give the pill but document it
Ask the client if she wants the extra pill
hha.exp: Medication administration is outside all HHA scope of practice regardless of who asks. The nurse determines how to handle a missed dose. Do not give medications.

hha.q: An HHA visits a client's home and notices the client has bruises on her arms in various stages of healing. The client seems afraid and says 'I fell.' The BEST action is:
choices: Accept the explanation and document 'client states she fell'
Report concerns about possible abuse to the supervising nurse immediately — HHAs are mandatory reporters
Ask the client directly: 'Is someone hitting you?'
Contact Adult Protective Services independently without notifying the supervisor first
hha.exp: Multiple bruises in various stages = possible abuse pattern. HHAs are mandatory reporters. Report to the supervisor who will engage the proper channels (APS, case management).

hha.q: At the end of a home visit, an HHA realizes she forgot to document the client's vital signs she took earlier. She should:
choices: Add them to yesterday's note since it is close enough
Document them now with the correct time they were taken and a late-entry note ('late entry — omitted from earlier documentation')
Fabricate approximate numbers
Ask the client to confirm the numbers from memory
hha.exp: Late entry documentation is acceptable and legal — clearly label it. Never falsify, alter, or fabricate documentation. Accurate records protect the client and the HHA.

hha.q: An HHA is asked by the client to be listed as a beneficiary in the client's will. The HHA should:
choices: Accept — the client can choose whoever they want
Decline and report to the supervising nurse — accepting gifts or inheritance from clients is a form of financial exploitation
Accept only if the amount is small
Ask the client to wait until after the care relationship ends
hha.exp: HHAs accepting money, gifts, or inheritance from clients is financial exploitation — a form of abuse and grounds for termination and criminal charges.

hha.q: An HHA arrives at a client's home to find the client unresponsive on the floor and not breathing. There is no DNR on file. The HHA should:
choices: Call the supervising nurse and wait for instructions
Call 911 immediately and begin CPR if trained
Leave to find a phone since cell service is unreliable
Check the care plan before doing anything
hha.exp: No DNR = CPR is indicated. Call 911 first (dispatchers can guide CPR). Start CPR if trained. In a home setting, 911 is always the first call for a life-threatening emergency.

hha.q: An HHA disagrees with a client's lifestyle choices (diet, smoking, religious practices). The BEST approach is:
choices: Gently educate the client about healthier choices during every visit
Refuse to provide care in areas related to behaviors she disapproves of
Respect the client's autonomy and personal choices while providing professional care without judgment
Report the lifestyle choices to the supervising nurse as a health risk
hha.exp: Client autonomy = the right to make their own choices about their own life. HHAs provide non-judgmental care regardless of personal beliefs about the client's choices.

teas.q: Homeostasis is best described as:
choices: The body's process for digestion
The body's ability to maintain a stable internal environment
The rate at which the heart beats
The process of breathing
teas.exp: Homeostasis = the body maintaining balance (stable temperature, pH, blood sugar, etc.) despite external changes.

teas.q: Alveoli are:
choices: Small bones in the ear
Tiny air sacs in the lungs where gas exchange occurs
White blood cells
Valves in the heart
teas.exp: Alveoli = tiny grape-like air sacs at the ends of the bronchioles. Oxygen crosses into the blood; CO₂ crosses out. This is gas exchange.

teas.q: Hemoglobin is an iron-containing protein that:
choices: Fights infections
Carries oxygen in red blood cells
Produces hormones
Digests fat
teas.exp: Hemoglobin in red blood cells binds O₂ in the lungs and releases it in tissues. Low hemoglobin = anemia = fatigue and pallor.

teas.q: A patient has low red blood cell count. Their hemoglobin will be ___, and they may appear ___.
choices: High / flushed
Low / pale and fatigued
Normal / unchanged
High / confused
teas.exp: Low RBC count = low hemoglobin = anemia. Less oxygen delivered to tissues = fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath.

teas.q: Cardiac muscle is found:
choices: In all muscles of the body
Only in the heart wall
In the walls of blood vessels
In skeletal muscles
teas.exp: Cardiac muscle = only in the heart. It is involuntary (you can't consciously control your heartbeat) and striated (has stripes under a microscope).

teas.q: The integumentary system is made up of:
choices: Heart, arteries, and veins
Skin, hair, and nails
Kidneys, bladder, and urethra
Brain, spinal cord, and nerves
teas.exp: Integumentary (from Latin integumentum = covering) = skin, hair, nails. The body's outermost protective layer.

teas.q: When you are too hot, your skin sweats. This is an example of:
choices: Pathogen response
Homeostasis — the body maintaining stable temperature
Cardiac output regulation
Respiratory rate adjustment
teas.exp: Sweating is a homeostatic response — the body uses evaporation to cool down and maintain its 98.6°F target temperature.

teas.q: DNA is found in which part of the cell?
choices: Cell membrane
Mitochondria
Nucleus
Ribosomes
teas.exp: DNA is stored in the cell's nucleus — the control center. The nucleus is surrounded by a nuclear membrane.

teas.q: Mitosis produces:
choices: Four genetically unique cells (sex cells)
Two genetically identical cells (for growth and repair)
One cell with half the chromosome number
Sperm and egg cells only
teas.exp: Mitosis = cell division for growth and repair. Produces 2 identical daughter cells (same chromosome number as parent). Meiosis = sex cell production.

teas.q: A skin cell divides to repair a wound. This is an example of:
choices: Meiosis
Mitosis
Natural selection
Binary fission
teas.exp: Wound healing = skin cells dividing to replace damaged tissue = mitosis. Meiosis only makes sex cells in the gonads.

teas.q: A chromosome is:
choices: A type of protein
A thread-like structure made of DNA that carries genes
A cell organelle that produces energy
A type of hormone
teas.exp: Chromosomes = coiled-up DNA. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Chromosomes carry the genes that determine traits.

teas.q: A compound is:
choices: A single element
Two or more elements chemically bonded together
A mixture of substances
A type of atom
teas.exp: Compound = chemical bond between 2+ elements. Water (H₂O) = hydrogen + oxygen. Salt (NaCl) = sodium + chlorine. Always in fixed ratios.

teas.q: The pH scale measures:
choices: Blood pressure
Temperature
Acidity or alkalinity (0=acidic, 14=basic, 7=neutral)
Electrical charge
teas.exp: pH: 0-6 = acidic, 7 = neutral, 8-14 = basic (alkaline). Stomach acid ≈ pH 2. Blood = pH 7.35-7.45 (slightly basic).

teas.q: A patient's blood pH is 7.28. This means their blood is:
choices: Too basic (alkalosis)
Normal
Too acidic (acidosis)
Not measurable
teas.exp: Normal blood pH = 7.35-7.45. pH 7.28 < 7.35 = acidosis. This is a dangerous imbalance requiring emergency treatment.

teas.q: ATP stands for and is the cell's:
choices: Amino Transfer Process — building block of proteins
Adenosine Triphosphate — the main energy currency of cells
Antigen Transport Protein — part of the immune system
Alveolar Transfer Pressure — a lung measurement
teas.exp: ATP = adenosine triphosphate. Produced during cellular respiration. Every cell process — muscle contraction, nerve impulse, protein synthesis — requires ATP.

teas.q: The prefix 'hypo-' means:
choices: Above normal
Below normal or deficient
Around or surrounding
Through or across
teas.exp: Hypo = below/under/deficient. Hypotension = low blood pressure. Hypothermia = low body temperature. Hypoglycemia = low blood sugar.

teas.q: The suffix '-itis' means:
choices: Removal of
Inflammation of
Study of
Disease of
teas.exp: -itis = inflammation. Arthritis = joint inflammation. Appendicitis = appendix inflammation. Bronchitis = bronchial inflammation.

teas.q: A patient with 'myalgia' is experiencing:
choices: Heart problems
Muscle pain
Kidney disease
Skin inflammation
teas.exp: My/myo = muscle. -algia = pain. Myalgia = muscle pain. Common after exercise or during viral illness.

teas.q: The word 'contraindicated' means:
choices: Recommended
Advised against / should NOT be done due to harm risk
Required
Uncertain
teas.exp: Contra = against. Indicated = recommended. Contraindicated = NOT recommended because it could cause harm.

teas.q: 'Ambulatory' means:
choices: Confused or disoriented
Able to walk independently
Attached to medical equipment
In pain
teas.exp: Ambulatory = able to walk. From Latin 'ambulare' = to walk. Non-ambulatory patients require assistance or wheelchair transport.

teas.q: What is the absolute value of -15?
teas.exp: Absolute value = distance from zero, always positive. |-15| = 15.

teas.q: What is 3/4 as a decimal?
50% is the same as:
teas.exp: 50% = 50/100 = 1/2 = 0.5. All three representations mean the same thing.

teas.q: How many milliliters are in 1 liter?
choices: 10 mL
100 mL
teas.exp: 1 liter = 1,000 milliliters. Milli = 1/1000. A standard water bottle is about 500 mL (half a liter).

teas.q: PEMDAS tells us to solve this order: 4 + 2 × 3 = ?
teas.exp: PEMDAS: Multiply first: 2×3=6. Then add: 4+6=10.

teas.q: Which of these is a verb?
choices: Hospital
Rapidly
Administer
Careful
teas.exp: A verb expresses action or state. 'Administer' is an action. Hospital (noun), Rapidly (adverb), Careful (adjective).

teas.q: In 'The patient walked slowly,' what part of speech is 'slowly'?
choices: Noun
Verb
Adjective
Adverb
teas.exp: 'Slowly' modifies the verb 'walked' — telling HOW the patient walked. Words that modify verbs/adjectives/other adverbs are adverbs.

teas.q: Choose the correct word: 'The doctor checked ___ patient chart.'
choices: they're
their
there
thier
teas.exp: Their = possessive (it belongs to them). Their patient's chart. They're = they are. There = a place.

teas.q: Choose the correct word: 'The medication will ___ the patient's pain.'
choices: effect
affect
affeck
efect
teas.exp: Affect = verb (to influence). Effect = noun (the result). 'Will affect' uses the verb form. 'The effect was positive' uses the noun form.

teas.q: The prefix 'pre-' means:
choices: After
Before
Against
Through
teas.exp: Pre- = before. Pre-operative = before surgery. Pre-existing = existed before. Precaution = action taken before a problem.

interpreter.q: Mr. Smith is ___________ in his job by his lack of education.
choices: hardened
hampered
hallowed
heightened
interpreter.exp: HAMPERED means hindered or held back. His lack of education holds him back. French: entravé, gêné.

interpreter.q: Studies have shown that longevity in the Far East is ______________ to the inhabitants' wholesome diet.
choices: attributed
discovered
disconnected
contributed
interpreter.exp: ATTRIBUTED TO means caused by or credited to. 'Longevity is attributed to their diet' = their diet explains their long lives. French: attribué à.

interpreter.q: Both spouses were ___________ about the division of their assets, as each felt they had contributed more to the accumulation of their wealth.
choices: unwavering
compliant
contrite
affable
interpreter.exp: UNWAVERING means firm, not changing position. Each spouse firmly held their own position. French: inébranlable, ferme.

interpreter.q: The military was secretly intent on removing the dictator, so the operation planned had to be _______.
choices: complex
vulnerable
transparent
covert
interpreter.exp: COVERT means secret, hidden. A secret military operation must be covert. French: secret, clandestin. KEY LEGAL WORD: used often in court (covert operation, covert surveillance).

interpreter.q: The judge gave the jury instructions in a ______________ manner because their comprehension was critical to the case.
choices: deliberate
feeble
callous
monotonous
interpreter.exp: DELIBERATE manner = slow, careful, purposeful. The judge was careful because understanding was critical. French: délibéré, mesuré, posé.

interpreter.q: A 'not guilty' verdict was almost __________ given the conflicting testimony of the undercover police officers.
choices: improbable
indefinite
inevitable
far-fetched
interpreter.exp: INEVITABLE means certain to happen, unavoidable. Conflicting police testimony made acquittal nearly certain. French: inévitable, inéluctable.

interpreter.q: The entertainer amused the crowd with his ________ mannerisms while performing his comedic routine.
choices: serious
jocular
distressful
frivolous
interpreter.exp: JOCULAR means humorous, joking, playful. A comedian has jocular mannerisms. French: jovial, facétieux, badin.

interpreter.q: The hiker's allergic reaction to bee stings caused him to be _________ after he was bitten by a swarm of bees.
choices: incapacitated
bemused
enabled
coerced
interpreter.exp: INCAPACITATED means unable to function, disabled. A severe allergic reaction incapacitates you. French: incapacité, rendu incapable. LEGAL: incapacitated witnesses, incapacitated defendants.

interpreter.q: The defense attorney did not consider his witness' reluctance to testify as a(n) ________ to his defense since the attorney had other witnesses who saw what happened.
choices: hindrance
asset
opposition
advantage
interpreter.exp: HINDRANCE means obstacle, something that holds you back. The attorney did NOT consider it a hindrance because he had other witnesses. French: obstacle, entrave. LEGAL: hindrance to justice.

interpreter.q: Although charged with grand larceny, the bank teller was careful not to ________ others who were involved in the crime for fear of retribution.
choices: oscillate
intonate
inundate
implicate
interpreter.exp: IMPLICATE means to involve or connect someone to a crime. He didn't want to name others for fear of retaliation. French: impliquer. KEY LEGAL WORD: 'the evidence implicates the defendant.'

interpreter.q: According to the passage about court interpreters, a criminal trial may be voided if:
choices: an interpreter is not provided where one is needed.
any of the parties do not speak English.
an interpreter was requested but was unavailable.
a physical disability such as a hearing impairment is involved.
interpreter.exp: The passage states: 'Failure to provide an interpreter where one is needed may void the outcome in a criminal trial.' Only when one is NEEDED and not PROVIDED.
interpreter.passage: People who appear in a court in the State of New York have the basic right to an interpreter. Criminal defendants must be allowed to assist in their own defense by being present at their trial and being able to understand the court proceedings. The courts have ruled that criminal defendants who cannot understand English are entitled to have the proceedings interpreted for them in a language that they do understand. Failure to provide an interpreter where one is needed may void the outcome in a criminal trial. Similarly, in a civil trial, where a witness or party does not speak English, the court has the authority to appoint an interpreter.

interpreter.q: According to the passage about court interpretation services, educational institutions should provide curricula about language interpretation services because:
choices: denying knowledge about interpretation would be equivalent to denying a constitutional right.
there is a veritable explosion of foreign immigrants coming to the U.S.
students would have more opportunities to find work by using their language skills.
municipalities and non-profit organizations all use interpreters.
interpreter.exp: The passage says curricula 'could open innumerable doors to many fulfilling career paths' — career opportunities are the stated reason. French: débouchés professionnels.
interpreter.passage: Local colleges and universities would be surprised to learn that providing their students with curricula that reinforce their linguistic skills could open innumerable doors to many fulfilling career paths.

interpreter.q: According to the passage, which of the following would benefit from having interpreters?
choices: city hospitals
law offices
hotels in high tourist areas
news organizations
interpreter.exp: The passage explicitly lists 'city hospitals' among the organizations vying for interpretation services. Law offices are not mentioned.

interpreter.q: According to the passage, which of the following statements is NOT correct?
choices: Immigrant communities in states other than New York have significantly increased.
Universities are actively engaged in developing interpreting departments.
Court systems nationwide recognize the constitutional right to an interpreter.
New York's leadership in this area has been embraced by other states.
interpreter.exp: The passage says universities have 'yet to fully embrace' language services curricula — meaning they are NOT actively engaged. This makes B false, which is the NOT correct statement.

interpreter.q: According to the passage about rent-stabilized apartments, which of the following statements is correct?
choices: Improvements in the economy were first noticed two years ago.
Real estate analysts believe vacancy rates are related to rent increases.
The developments planned in the near future will help tenants find bigger apartments.
As the economy began to improve, landlords offered fewer incentives.
interpreter.exp: The passage says 'as the economy began to rebound, landlords have been able to demand higher rents and are less willing to provide these types of incentives.' D is correct.
interpreter.passage: As the economy began to rebound, landlords have been able to demand higher rents and are less willing to provide these types of incentives. Real estate analysts now say that several factors are conspiring to keep rents high. They cite the low vacancy rates, the shortage of new developments on the horizon and the increased demand.

interpreter.q: According to the passage about apartment rentals, which of the following statements is NOT correct?
choices: Paying over $2,000.00 for a two-bedroom apartment was reasonable 2 years ago.
The rebounding economy has led to a shortage of new developments.
Landlords today are exploring incentives to attract tenants.
Luxury apartment buildings offered leases with 1 month of free rent.
interpreter.exp: The passage says landlords are LESS WILLING to provide incentives now — C says they ARE exploring incentives, which is the opposite of what the passage states.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: While the victim lay injured on the floor of the store, the police dispatcher counseled the shopkeeper on how to perform first aid.
While the victim laid injured on the floor of the store, the police dispatcher concealed the shopkeeper on how to perform first aid.
While the victim lay injured on the floor of the store, the police dispatcher counciled the shopkeeper on how to perform first aid.
While the victim laid injured on the floor of the store, the police dispatcher cancelled the shopkeeper on how to perform first aid.
interpreter.exp: LAY (not laid) is the past tense of 'lie' (to recline). COUNSELED (not counciled) = advised. A is the only sentence with both correct words.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: Cable companies has expanded into the communications arena.
The police department operator was unabled to hear the caller due to static.
Stunning advances in medicine are keeping people alive well into their nineties.
She and I have never agree on anything of significant importance.
interpreter.exp: C is correct. A: 'companies has' → wrong subject-verb agreement. B: 'unabled' is not a word. D: 'have never agree' → should be 'agreed.'

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: I wonder how much costs that book.
I wonder what of that book the price is.
I wonder how much does that book cost.
I wonder what that book costs.
interpreter.exp: In indirect questions (after 'I wonder'), normal word order is used — no inversion. 'I wonder what that book costs' (not 'costs that book' or 'does that book cost'). French: Je me demande combien coûte ce livre.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: There is a reason why drivers and passengers should have seatbelts securely fastened.
Seatbelt fastened is known to prevent injuries.
Highway patrolman see many fatal accidents without seatbelts fastened.
Speed limits and seatbelts fastened are their to reduce fatal injuries.
interpreter.exp: A is correct. B: 'Seatbelt fastened' is not standard. C: 'patrolman see' should be 'patrolmen see.' D: 'their' should be 'there.'

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: If you do not have a lawyer, filing a claim which are complex can be intimidating.
If you do not have a lawyer, filing a claim which is complex can be intimidating.
If you do not have lawyers filing a claim which are complex, can be intimidating.
If you do not have a lawyer filing a claim which is complex, can be intimidating.
interpreter.exp: B is correct. 'A claim which IS complex' (singular) — subject-verb agreement. The relative clause modifies 'a claim' (singular), so 'is' is correct.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: Disappointed baseball fans had mix feelings as they look forward to football games.
Ticket prices are expected to encourage perspective fans.
Watching the recent draft picks play is the most exciting part of the game.
Icy roads should of discouraged me from driving and getting into an accident.
interpreter.exp: C is correct. A: 'mix feelings' → mixed feelings. B: 'perspective' → prospective. D: 'should of' → should have.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: According to a common belief, Europe and Asia are regarded as being distinct continents, but they are vast geographic divisions of the larger land mass known as Eurasia.
It is according to a common belief, Europe and Asia are regarded as distinct continents, but they are vast geographic divisions of the larger land mass known as Eurasia.
That it is according to a common belief, Europe and Asia are regarded as being distinct continents, but they are vast geographic divisions of the larger land mass known as Eurasia.
While according to a common belief, Europe and Asia are regarded as being distinct continents, but they are vast geographic divisions of the larger land mass known as Eurasia.
interpreter.exp: A is correct. B, C, D all create awkward sentence structures. A starts cleanly with 'According to' as an introductory phrase.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: Public transportation in most of the nation is expanding. Although, the use of subways and buses is declining in some metropolitan areas.
Public transportation in most of the nation is expanding. Consequently, the use of subways and buses is declining in some metropolitan areas.
Public transportation in most of the nation is expanding. Hence, the use of subways and buses is declining in some metropolitan areas.
Public transportation in most of the nation is expanding. Nevertheless, the use of subways and buses is declining in some metropolitan areas.
interpreter.exp: NEVERTHELESS = despite that, however. Expansion overall + decline in some areas = a contrast → use 'Nevertheless.' Consequently/Hence would mean the decline IS BECAUSE OF the expansion, which is wrong. French: néanmoins.

interpreter.q: Choose the grammatically correct sentence:
choices: Between you and I, she always tells the truth.
Between you and me, she always tells the truth.
Between you and myself, she always tells the truth.
Between yourself and I, she always tells the truth.
interpreter.exp: After prepositions (between, with, for, to), use objective pronouns: ME, HIM, HER, US, THEM. Never 'I' after a preposition. 'Between you and ME' — not 'I' or 'myself.'

interpreter.q: LABYRINTH most closely means:
choices: flower
building
maze
overwhelming
interpreter.exp: LABYRINTH = a complex network of paths; a maze. Origin: Greek mythology (the Minotaur's maze). French: labyrinthe. Used in court: 'a labyrinth of regulations.'

interpreter.q: PARDON most closely means:
choices: appease
placate
restore
forgive
interpreter.exp: PARDON = to forgive, to excuse. KEY LEGAL WORD: a presidential or gubernatorial pardon forgives a criminal sentence. French: pardonner, grâce (presidential pardon = grâce présidentielle).

interpreter.q: VERACITY most closely means:
choices: aggressiveness
truthfulness
full of life
provability
interpreter.exp: VERACITY = truthfulness, accuracy. Root: Latin 'veritas' (truth). French: véracité. LEGAL: 'the veracity of the witness' = whether the witness is telling the truth.

interpreter.q: ONUS most closely means:
choices: burden
grace
dignity
bias
interpreter.exp: ONUS = burden, responsibility, duty. KEY LEGAL PHRASE: 'onus probandi' = burden of proof. French: fardeau, charge. 'The onus is on the prosecution to prove guilt.'

interpreter.q: QUIBBLE most closely means:
choices: shiver
acquiesce
submit
argue
interpreter.exp: QUIBBLE = to argue about minor details, to find petty objections. French: chicaner, ergoter. In court: 'Don't quibble over definitions — focus on the evidence.'

interpreter.q: PREY most closely means:
choices: animal
victor
primal
victim
interpreter.exp: PREY = victim; one who is hunted or exploited. French: proie, victime. In legal context: 'the criminal preyed on elderly victims.'

interpreter.q: APPEASE most closely means:
choices: excite
agitate
placate
curtail
interpreter.exp: APPEASE = to pacify, calm, satisfy. PLACATE = same meaning. French: apaiser, calmer. Both words mean to make someone less angry or upset.

interpreter.q: BELLICOSE most closely means:
choices: meditative
combative
compliant
passive
interpreter.exp: BELLICOSE = aggressive, warlike, ready to fight. Root: Latin 'bellum' (war). French: belliqueux, combatif. 'A bellicose defendant disrupted the courtroom.'

interpreter.q: PLUNDER most closely means:
choices: loot
replace
confiscate
succeed
interpreter.exp: PLUNDER = to loot, to steal by force, to rob. French: piller, saccager. Distinguished from confiscate (legal seizure by authorities).

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of IMPEDE is:
choices: obstruct
facilitate
distribute
interfere
interpreter.exp: IMPEDE = to block, hinder, slow down. Opposite: FACILITATE = to make easier, to help move forward. French: Impede = entraver / Facilitate = faciliter. KEY LEGAL: 'obstruction of justice' vs 'facilitating a crime.'

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of FELONIOUS is:
choices: angelic
cat-like
satanic
criminal
interpreter.exp: FELONIOUS = criminal, relating to a felony. Opposite: ANGELIC = innocent, pure, law-abiding (the closest opposite given). French: félonieux = criminel / angélique = innocent. KEY LEGAL: felony vs misdemeanor.

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of MOURN is:
choices: lament
grieve
celebrate
renew
interpreter.exp: MOURN = to grieve, to be sad about a loss. Opposite: CELEBRATE = to rejoice. French: pleurer ≠ célébrer.

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of DEPLETE is:
choices: exhaust
replenish
eradicate
consume
interpreter.exp: DEPLETE = to use up, to drain, to exhaust. Opposite: REPLENISH = to refill, to restore. French: épuiser ≠ réapprovisionner.

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of AFFIRM is:
choices: declare
witness
support
negate
interpreter.exp: AFFIRM = to confirm, to state positively. Opposite: NEGATE = to deny, to cancel out. French: affirmer ≠ nier. KEY LEGAL: affirmative defense, negation of evidence.

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of DELIBERATE is:
choices: motivational
hurried
reflective
intentional
interpreter.exp: DELIBERATE = done on purpose, carefully planned, slow and methodical. Opposite: HURRIED = rushed, without care. French: délibéré ≠ précipité, hâtif.

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of JOVIAL is:
choices: ecstatic
furious
jolly
sedative
interpreter.exp: JOVIAL = cheerful, good-humored, happy. Opposite: FURIOUS = extremely angry. French: jovial ≠ furieux. Note: 'jolly' is a synonym of jovial, not an antonym.

interpreter.q: The OPPOSITE of EXTOL is:
choices: applaud
communicate
glorify
criticize
interpreter.exp: EXTOL = to praise highly, to glorify, to applaud. Opposite: CRITICIZE = to find fault, to censure. French: exalter, glorifier ≠ critiquer.

interpreter.q: 'Sharp as a tack' means:
choices: difficult to get along with
pointy
dangerous
very bright
interpreter.exp: Sharp as a tack = very intelligent, mentally quick. A tack (small nail) is sharp. French equivalent: 'avoir l'esprit vif' (to have a quick mind). Context: 'That interpreter is sharp as a tack — she catches every nuance.'

interpreter.q: 'Swamped' means:
choices: overwhelmed
waterlogged
sticky
crowded
interpreter.exp: Swamped = completely overwhelmed with work or tasks. Like being sunk in a swamp. French: submergé, débordé de travail. 'The public defender was swamped with cases.'

interpreter.q: 'Dodged a bullet' means:
choices: hid in a tight space
avoided a problem
resolved an argument
unscrambled a code
interpreter.exp: Dodged a bullet = narrowly avoided something bad. French: l'échapper belle, éviter de justesse. 'He dodged a bullet when charges were dropped at the last minute.'

interpreter.q: 'Playing the devil's advocate' means:
choices: consorting with the enemy
twisting the truth
putting forth counter-arguments
enacting satanic rituals
interpreter.exp: Devil's advocate = someone who argues the opposite side to test an argument, even if they don't believe it. French: l'avocat du diable. Common in legal preparation: attorneys play devil's advocate to test their case.

interpreter.q: 'Dry run' means:
choices: jogging in clear weather
sun after a rain shower
parched speech
rehearsal
interpreter.exp: Dry run = a rehearsal or practice run before the real event. French: répétition générale, exercice. 'Let's do a dry run of the interpretation before the actual hearing.'

interpreter.q: 'A smoking gun' means:
choices: a gun that backfires
obvious proof of guilt
the weapon used in a crime
an angry person
interpreter.exp: Smoking gun = clear, undeniable evidence of wrongdoing. French: preuve irréfutable. From the image of a freshly fired gun still smoking at a crime scene. Heavily used in legal news coverage.

interpreter.q: 'Cold turkey' means:
choices: abruptly
incrementally
when eating fowl
after meals
interpreter.exp: Cold turkey = to stop something suddenly and completely. French: brutalement, d'un seul coup. 'He quit cold turkey' = he stopped immediately without gradually reducing. Used in addiction/rehabilitation discussions in court.

interpreter.q: 'In a nutshell' means:
choices: in a confined place
in summary
in lieu of
in quotations
interpreter.exp: In a nutshell = briefly, in a few words, in summary. French: en bref, en résumé, pour résumer. 'In a nutshell, the defendant has no alibi.'

interpreter.q: 'The grass is always greener on the other side' means:
choices: The grass grows on the sunny side of the street.
Circumstances may change.
Some other place is always better than where you are.
There is more than one path to success.
interpreter.exp: The grass is always greener = other people's situations always seem better than yours. French: L'herbe est toujours plus verte chez le voisin (direct equivalent in French!). Common human tendency to idealize what we don't have.

interpreter.q: 'Don't cry over spilled milk' means:
choices: Do not regret what cannot be changed.
Focusing your hopes on one person is a waste.
Plan for the future.
Spilled milk is spoiled milk.
interpreter.exp: Don't cry over spilled milk = don't waste time regretting past mistakes that can't be fixed. French: Ce qui est fait est fait (what's done is done). Important for court: clients often dwell on what they could have done differently.

interpreter.q: 'Have your cake and eat it too' means:
choices: Eating cake will make you sick
Enjoy it while it lasts
Don't regret past events
Wanting it both ways
interpreter.exp: Have your cake and eat it too = wanting two incompatible things at the same time; wanting it both ways. French: vouloir le beurre et l'argent du beurre (wanting the butter and the money from the butter). 'The defendant wants a reduced sentence but also no probation — you can't have your cake and eat it too.'

interpreter.q: The instructions that a judge gives to the jury before the jury begins deliberations is known as the:
choices: verdict
closing argument
judge's charge
summation
interpreter.exp: JUDGE'S CHARGE = the formal instructions a judge gives the jury explaining the law they must apply. French: instructions au jury, charge du juge. Distinct from closing arguments (made by attorneys) and verdict (the jury's decision).

interpreter.q: A monetary amount for conditional pretrial release from custody is known as:
choices: lien
bail
fine
surcharge
interpreter.exp: BAIL = money deposited as security to ensure the defendant appears in court; allows pretrial release. French: caution, liberté sous caution. Bail bond, bail hearing, bail jumping are common court terms.

interpreter.q: To oust someone from a dwelling through the legal process is to:
choices: dispossess
disenfranchise
disintegrate
disassemble
interpreter.exp: DISPOSSESS = to legally remove a tenant from property; eviction. French: expulser, évincer. DISENFRANCHISE = to take away voting rights. Important distinction for interpreters serving in housing court.

interpreter.q: An admission of guilt in exchange for a specific sentence is:
choices: a plea bargain
a hung jury
a no lo contendere
ipso facto
interpreter.exp: PLEA BARGAIN = agreement where defendant pleads guilty to lesser charge in exchange for reduced sentence. French: accord de culpabilité, négociation de peine. The most common way US criminal cases are resolved (~90% of convictions).

interpreter.q: A 'retainer' is an agreement between:
choices: two or more jurors
a juror and a judge
a client and an attorney
two or more attorneys
interpreter.exp: RETAINER = a fee paid in advance to an attorney to secure their services; also the agreement itself. French: provision (d'honoraires), honoraires de retenue. 'The client paid a $5,000 retainer to the defense attorney.'

interpreter.q: A conviction happens when:
choices: the prosecutor's evidence is overwhelming
the defense has an ironclad alibi
all the witnesses are credible
the jury returns a guilty verdict
interpreter.exp: CONVICTION = a formal declaration that someone is guilty, specifically when the jury returns a guilty verdict. French: condamnation, verdict de culpabilité. Evidence being overwhelming is a factor but not the definition — only the verdict matters.

interpreter.q: Legal assistance which is free of charge is:
choices: non pro tunc
pro se
pro bono
de novo
interpreter.exp: PRO BONO = Latin for 'for the good' (pro bono publico = for the public good); free legal work. French: pro bono, bénévole. PRO SE = representing yourself. DE NOVO = new trial. NON PRO TUNC = not as of a past date (rare term).

interpreter.q: Offenders who repeatedly commit the same crimes are involved in:
choices: restitution
res ipsa loquitur
reciprocity
recidivism
interpreter.exp: RECIDIVISM = the tendency of convicted criminals to re-offend. Root: Latin 'recidivus' (falling back). French: récidive. 'A high recidivism rate' = many released prisoners commit crimes again. KEY word for sentencing discussions.

interpreter.q: A judgment awarding a plaintiff relief sought in the complaint because the defendant failed to appear in court is known as a:
choices: judgment by comparison
judgment of dismissal
default judgment
judgment of discontinuance
interpreter.exp: DEFAULT JUDGMENT = judgment entered against a party who fails to respond or appear in court. French: jugement par défaut. Common in civil cases when defendants ignore summons. The plaintiff gets what they asked for automatically.

interpreter.q: A peremptory challenge allows an attorney to dismiss a potential juror:
choices: without cause or giving reason
with cause and giving reason
after a hearing is held
when everyone agrees in chambers
interpreter.exp: PEREMPTORY CHALLENGE = right to dismiss a juror without giving any reason (limited number per side). Different from 'challenge for cause' (must give a reason). French: récusation péremptoire vs récusation motivée. KEY procedural term in jury selection (voir dire).

interpreter.q: The United States has two parallel court systems. What are they?
choices: Civil courts and criminal courts
Federal courts and state courts
District courts and appellate courts
Local courts and national courts
interpreter.exp: The US has a federal court system (for federal laws and the US Constitution) and a separate state court system in each of the 50 states. An interpreter may work in either. Federal courts include US District Courts, US Courts of Appeals, and the US Supreme Court.

interpreter.q: What is the difference between a CIVIL case and a CRIMINAL case?
choices: Civil cases involve violence; criminal cases do not
Civil cases are between two private parties; criminal cases are brought by the government against a defendant
Civil cases are heard in federal court; criminal cases in state court
Civil cases are faster; criminal cases always go to a jury
interpreter.exp: In a civil case, one private party (person or company) sues another. The government is not a party. In a criminal case, the government (city, state, or federal) charges a person with breaking the law. An interpreter must understand which type of proceeding they are in.

interpreter.q: In a criminal case, what is the standard of proof the prosecution must meet?
choices: Preponderance of the evidence (more likely than not)
Clear and convincing evidence
Beyond a reasonable doubt
Probable cause
interpreter.exp: 'Beyond a reasonable doubt' is the highest legal standard and is required for criminal convictions. Civil cases use 'preponderance of the evidence' (more likely than not — over 50%). An interpreter should know these phrases precisely, as they are central to verdict explanations.

interpreter.q: What is a GRAND JURY?
choices: The jury that decides guilt or innocence at trial
A group of judges who review appeals
A group of citizens that decides whether there is enough evidence to formally charge someone with a felony
The jury for high-profile cases only
interpreter.exp: A grand jury reviews evidence presented by the prosecutor and decides whether there is probable cause to issue an indictment (formal charges). Grand jury proceedings are secret and there is no defense attorney present. The trial jury (petit jury) is the one that decides guilt at trial.

interpreter.q: What is an ARRAIGNMENT?
choices: The final verdict hearing
A hearing where the defendant is formally read the charges and enters a plea
The same as sentencing
A hearing to select jurors
interpreter.exp: At arraignment, the judge reads the formal charges to the defendant, who then enters a plea: guilty, not guilty, or no contest (nolo contendere). The court may also set bail at this hearing. Arraignment is often one of the first hearings in a criminal case.

interpreter.q: What is VOIR DIRE?
choices: The reading of the verdict
The process of questioning potential jurors to select an impartial jury
A French term for the opening statement
The cross-examination of witnesses
interpreter.exp: Voir dire (from French: 'to speak the truth') is the process of questioning potential jurors by the judge and attorneys to identify bias and select a fair jury. Interpreters are rarely needed during voir dire, but should understand the process.

interpreter.q: What does 'plaintiff' mean?
choices: The person accused of a crime
The judge in a civil case
The party who files a lawsuit in a civil case
The government prosecutor
interpreter.exp: In a civil case, the plaintiff is the party who files the lawsuit — the one claiming harm or damages. The other party is the defendant. In criminal cases, the equivalent is the prosecution (the government). Interpreters must render these role distinctions accurately.

interpreter.q: A defendant is found 'not guilty.' What does this mean legally?
choices: The court proved the defendant is innocent
The prosecution did not prove guilt beyond a reasonable doubt — not the same as proven innocent
The defendant can never be charged with the same crime again for any reason
The charges are suspended, not dismissed
interpreter.exp: Not guilty means the prosecution failed to meet the burden of proof. It is NOT the same as a declaration of innocence — it means the standard of 'beyond a reasonable doubt' was not met. Double jeopardy (5th Amendment) prevents retrial for the same offense in the same jurisdiction.

interpreter.q: What is PERJURY?
choices: Refusing to testify in court
Testifying in a language other than English without an interpreter
Lying under oath in a legal proceeding
Missing a court date
interpreter.exp: Perjury is the crime of knowingly making a false statement under oath in a legal proceeding. It is a felony. As an interpreter, you take an oath to interpret accurately — making intentional errors could constitute obstruction of justice.

interpreter.q: What is a SUBPOENA?
choices: A formal charge for a felony crime
A court order requiring a person to appear, testify, or produce documents
A written settlement agreement between two parties
The bail amount set by a judge
interpreter.exp: A subpoena (Latin: 'under penalty') compels a person to appear in court or produce documents. Failure to comply is contempt of court. A 'subpoena ad testificandum' requires testimony; a 'subpoena duces tecum' requires production of records. Interpreters may be subpoenaed.

interpreter.q: What is BAIL?
choices: A fine paid after conviction
A sum of money deposited to secure a defendant's release from custody until trial
An agreement to plead guilty in exchange for a reduced sentence
The right to remain silent
interpreter.exp: Bail is money or property deposited with the court to ensure the defendant appears for future court dates. If they appear, bail is returned. If they flee (skip bail), the money is forfeited. Judges consider flight risk and public safety when setting bail.

interpreter.q: What is a PLEA BARGAIN?
choices: A request for a mistrial
An agreement where the defendant pleads guilty to a lesser charge in exchange for a reduced sentence
The defendant's opening statement at trial
A deal where the victim drops the charges
interpreter.exp: A plea bargain (or plea deal) is a negotiated agreement between the prosecutor and defense where the defendant pleads guilty to a lesser charge or receives a reduced sentence. Over 90% of criminal cases in the US are resolved through plea bargains rather than trial.

interpreter.q: What does 'contempt of court' mean?
choices: Disagreeing with the judge's ruling
Behavior that disrespects or disobeys the authority of the court — punishable by fine or imprisonment
A verdict of not guilty in a criminal case
An objection raised by the defense attorney
interpreter.exp: Contempt of court covers disrespectful behavior (talking out of turn, refusing to answer questions) or disobeying court orders. Interpreters must maintain decorum at all times. A party who refuses to answer a question through an interpreter can be held in contempt.

interpreter.q: What is an AFFIDAVIT?
choices: An oral statement made in court
A written statement of facts confirmed under oath
A court order to arrest someone
A charge sheet listing criminal offenses
interpreter.exp: An affidavit is a written sworn statement. The person making it (the affiant) signs it in front of a notary or court officer, swearing the contents are true. Affidavits are used as evidence in many legal proceedings. Interpreters may be asked to assist in preparing affidavits.

interpreter.q: What does 'overruled' mean when a judge says it after an attorney's objection?
choices: The judge agrees with the objection and the evidence is excluded
The judge disagrees with the objection — the evidence or testimony is allowed
The case is dismissed
Both attorneys must stop talking
interpreter.exp: When a judge says 'overruled,' they are rejecting the objection — the evidence, question, or testimony may proceed. 'Sustained' means the judge agrees with the objection and the evidence is excluded. Interpreters must render these rulings quickly and accurately.

interpreter.q: What is a RESTRAINING ORDER (also called a Protective Order)?
choices: A warrant for a suspect's arrest
A court order prohibiting a person from contacting or coming near another person
An order freezing a person's bank accounts
An order requiring a witness to testify
interpreter.exp: A restraining order (or order of protection) prohibits a named person from contacting, approaching, or threatening the protected party. Violating a restraining order is a criminal offense. Interpreters often work in domestic violence cases where these orders are issued.

interpreter.q: What is the difference between 'hearsay' and direct testimony?
choices: Hearsay is testimony given in writing; direct testimony is spoken
Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered to prove the truth of its contents; direct testimony is what the witness personally saw or heard
Hearsay is allowed; direct testimony requires cross-examination
There is no legal difference between them
interpreter.exp: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered as evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted. It is generally inadmissible (with many exceptions). Direct testimony is what the witness personally observed. Interpreters must understand this distinction because hearsay objections frequently arise during interpreted testimony.

interpreter.q: What is an INDICTMENT?
choices: A guilty verdict returned by a trial jury
A formal written charge issued by a grand jury stating there is enough evidence to try someone for a felony
A sentencing recommendation from the prosecutor
A motion to dismiss the case
interpreter.exp: An indictment is a formal accusation by a grand jury that probable cause exists to charge someone with a felony. Once indicted, the defendant is arraigned on the indictment. Misdemeanor charges typically go through a 'complaint' or 'information' — not a grand jury.

interpreter.q: What does the Miranda warning inform a suspect?
choices: Their right to a speedy trial
That they have the right to remain silent, that anything they say can be used against them, and that they have the right to an attorney
The specific charges being filed against them
That they must appear in court at a certain date
interpreter.exp: The Miranda warning (from Miranda v. Arizona, 1966) must be read before custodial interrogation. It covers: right to remain silent; anything said can be used against you; right to an attorney; if you can't afford one, one will be appointed. In NYC, Miranda warnings must be given in the suspect's language.

interpreter.q: What is 'due process'?
choices: The right to a speedy trial
The constitutional guarantee of fair treatment and legal rights before the government can deprive you of life, liberty, or property
The process for filing a complaint with a court clerk
The steps an interpreter follows to certify their qualifications
interpreter.exp: Due process (5th and 14th Amendments) guarantees that government must follow fair procedures before depriving anyone of life, liberty, or property. It includes the right to notice, the right to be heard, and the right to a fair decision. Language access is part of due process — an unintelligible proceeding violates due process.

interpreter.q: According to NAJIT (National Association of Judiciary Interpreters and Translators), a court interpreter's PRIMARY obligation is to:
choices: Assist the defendant in understanding their legal options
Render complete, accurate, and faithful interpretations without adding, omitting, or changing anything
Summarize lengthy testimony to save time
Help the judge understand the cultural background of the LEP party
interpreter.exp: NAJIT's Code of Professional Responsibility (Canon 1) requires complete, accurate, and faithful renditions. The interpreter renders what is said — not what they think should be said. Additions, omissions, explanations, and clarifications made without instruction from the court are all prohibited.

interpreter.q: Which of the three standard modes of court interpretation involves the interpreter speaking simultaneously with the person being interpreted?
choices: Consecutive interpretation
Sight translation
Simultaneous interpretation
Summary interpretation
interpreter.exp: Simultaneous interpretation means speaking (or whispering) a translation at the same time as the speaker, with only a few seconds' delay. It is typically used for defendants during trial proceedings. Consecutive interpretation — waiting for the speaker to finish — is used for witness testimony and interviews.

interpreter.q: A defense attorney asks you to summarize (not fully interpret) the judge's long instructions to save time. You should:
choices: Summarize as requested — the attorney is in charge
Refuse and continue providing complete interpretations as required by professional standards
Ask the judge to repeat the instructions more slowly
Provide a partial interpretation covering only the most important points
interpreter.exp: Interpreters must provide complete, accurate interpretations at all times. Summary interpretation is not a recognized mode in formal court settings and violates professional standards. If asked to do so, the interpreter should politely remind the attorney of the interpreter's professional obligation.

interpreter.q: During a court recess, the defendant's family approaches you and asks you to explain what happened in court. You should:
choices: Explain what happened — this is a humanitarian duty
Explain the procedural parts but not the substance
Decline — the interpreter's role ends when the judge is not present
Refer them to the defendant's attorney and decline to discuss the case
interpreter.exp: Interpreters must maintain strict impartiality and avoid any private communication about the case. Speaking to parties during recess — even informally — creates the appearance of bias and may compromise the integrity of the proceeding. All questions should be directed to the attorney of record.

interpreter.q: You are assigned to interpret in a criminal case involving acts you find morally deeply disturbing. You should:
choices: Refuse the assignment immediately
Request a different case from the court coordinator before proceedings begin if distress would impair your accuracy
Continue but intentionally soften the worst testimony
Ask the attorney to not ask questions about those specific facts
interpreter.exp: If a case involves content that you believe will genuinely impair your ability to interpret accurately (not just discomfort), you should raise this with the court coordinator BEFORE the proceeding begins — not during. You may never alter testimony to soften it; that is a serious ethics violation.

interpreter.q: A certified court interpreter should refer to themselves in the:
choices: First person ('I') — interpreting everything the parties say as if speaking for themselves
Third person ('the interpreter') for clarity
Second person to avoid confusion
Both first and third person depending on the context
interpreter.exp: Court interpreters interpret in the first person — if the witness says 'I was at the store,' the interpreter says 'I was at the store,' not 'He says he was at the store.' First-person interpretation preserves the speaker's voice and helps the jury evaluate credibility directly.

interpreter.q: You are interpreting and realize mid-testimony that you made an error three sentences ago. You should:
choices: Ignore it and continue — stopping would confuse the record
Wait until the end of testimony to mention it
Immediately alert the court: 'Interpreter correction' and provide the correct interpretation
Ask the witness to repeat the entire testimony from the beginning
interpreter.exp: Accuracy requires correcting errors immediately. The professional standard is to say 'Interpreter's correction' or 'May the interpreter correct the record' and then provide the accurate version. Delaying corrections or ignoring them compromises the accuracy of the court record.

interpreter.q: Sight translation is:
choices: Translating a document orally, in real time, while reading it
Interpreting sign language for a deaf party
Translating a written document into a written document
Reading a court judgment aloud in English to the LEP party
interpreter.exp: Sight translation means reading a written document and rendering it orally in another language in real time. It is used when documents (complaint, plea, verdict) must be read to an LEP party during proceedings. It requires a different skill set from consecutive or simultaneous interpretation.

interpreter.q: An attorney asks you to help them understand their client's background and explain cultural context. You should:
choices: Help freely — cultural competence is part of the interpreter's role
Provide cultural context only if the judge specifically requests it
Decline — cultural brokering and case advice are outside the interpreter's role; refer to the attorney
Write a report to the court explaining the cultural factors
interpreter.exp: Court interpreters are NOT cultural brokers or legal advisors. Their role is linguistic — rendering speech faithfully. Providing cultural context, explaining background, or advising a party on strategy all compromise impartiality and are outside the scope of the court interpreter's role.

interpreter.q: If you feel you lack sufficient expertise to interpret a highly technical medical or scientific term during testimony, you should:
choices: Make your best guess and continue
Ask the witness to use simpler language
Alert the court immediately that you need clarification on a technical term
Skip the term and continue interpreting the rest
interpreter.exp: Interpreters must flag any gap in comprehension immediately. Saying 'May the interpreter ask for clarification on the technical term [X]?' is professional and required. Guessing or skipping terms violates the accuracy standard and may seriously affect the outcome of testimony.

interpreter.q: Can an interpreter who is also a bilingual attorney represent the LEP party AND interpret?
choices: Yes — it saves time and resources
Yes — but only in minor cases
No — a person cannot serve as both interpreter and attorney in the same case
Only if the judge grants permission
interpreter.exp: The roles of interpreter and attorney (or any other participant) are incompatible in the same case. An interpreter cannot simultaneously serve as an advocate. This is both an ethics violation and a conflict of interest that could void proceedings.

interpreter.q: NYS Judiciary Law §390 requires court interpreters to:
choices: Speak English as their native language
Be certified by the federal government
Take an oath to 'faithfully interpret' before interpreting in court
Work only in cases involving immigrants
interpreter.exp: NYS Judiciary Law §390 specifically requires that any person interpreting in a New York court must take an oath to faithfully interpret. This is in addition to any certification requirements. Interpreting without taking this oath could render proceedings voidable.

interpreter.q: NYC Executive Order 120 (2008) requires:
choices: All NYC residents to speak English in city offices
All City agencies to provide free language access services to people with limited English proficiency (LEP)
Only courts to provide interpreters
Federal agencies operating in NYC to provide translators
interpreter.exp: NYC Executive Order 120, signed by Mayor Bloomberg in 2008, requires all City agencies with significant LEP populations to provide language access services — including oral interpretation and written translations — at no cost to the individual. This applies to agencies like NYPD, ACS, HRA, DOB, and many others.

interpreter.q: Under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act (1964), what are recipients of federal funding (hospitals, courts, schools) required to do for LEP individuals?
choices: Provide an English class before services are rendered
Take reasonable steps to ensure meaningful access for people with limited English proficiency
Hire only certified interpreters for all encounters
Charge LEP individuals for interpretation services
interpreter.exp: Title VI prohibits discrimination based on national origin. DOJ guidance (2000) clarifies that failing to provide language access to LEP individuals constitutes national origin discrimination. Any entity receiving federal funds — hospitals, schools, courts, social services — must provide meaningful language access.

interpreter.q: The NYC Human Rights Law (Admin Code §8-107) protects against discrimination based on:
choices: Only race and religion
National origin, which includes language discrimination
Only immigration status
Language discrimination only when intentional
interpreter.exp: The NYC Human Rights Law, one of the strongest in the nation, prohibits discrimination in employment, housing, and public accommodations based on national origin — which courts have interpreted to include language. It is enforced by the NYC Commission on Human Rights.

interpreter.q: NYC Health + Hospitals (HHC) policy on language access requires:
choices: Patients to bring their own interpreter
Free, competent interpretation services for all patients with LEP, including 24-hour telephone interpretation
Family members to interpret all medical information
English-only communication in clinical settings
interpreter.exp: NYC Health + Hospitals provides free language services to all patients, including in-person medical interpreters, telephone interpretation 24/7 (via AT&T Language Line or equivalent), and translated vital documents. Using family members to interpret medical information — especially children — is discouraged as it raises accuracy and privacy concerns.

interpreter.q: Under DOJ guidance, what are the FOUR factors used to determine whether a language access program is adequate?
choices: Cost, speed, accuracy, and availability
Number of LEP persons served, frequency of contact, importance of the program, and resources available
Staff size, language diversity, budget, and location
Federal mandate, state mandate, local mandate, and volunteer capacity
interpreter.exp: The DOJ '4-factor analysis' (from the 2002 LEP Guidance) evaluates: (1) the number/proportion of LEP persons in the service area, (2) the frequency with which LEP persons come into contact with the program, (3) the importance of the program to LEP persons' lives, and (4) the resources available. This determines how robust language access must be.

interpreter.q: Which NYC agency certifies court interpreters for the New York State Unified Court System?
choices: NYC Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs (MOIA)
NYC Commission on Human Rights
NYS Office of Court Administration (OCA)
NYC Department of Citywide Administrative Services
interpreter.exp: The NYS Office of Court Administration (OCA) administers the New York State Court Interpreter Certification Program. Certified interpreters must pass both written and oral exams. OCA also oversees language access compliance in all state courts under the Court Interpreter Program.

interpreter.q: What does 'LEP' stand for in the context of language access law?
choices: Legal English Proficiency
Limited English Proficiency — a person who does not speak English as their primary language and who has limited ability to read, write, speak, or understand English
Language-Equivalent Participant
Language Equity Program
interpreter.exp: LEP (Limited English Proficiency) is the legal term used in Title VI, Executive Order 13166, and NYC EO 120 to describe individuals who need language assistance. An LEP person has a constitutional and civil rights-based right to meaningful access in federally funded programs.

interpreter.q: Federal Executive Order 13166 (2000), signed by President Clinton, requires:
choices: All federal agencies to hire bilingual employees
Federal agencies and recipients of federal assistance to improve language access for LEP persons
English as the official language of federal courts
Certified interpreters only in federal criminal cases
interpreter.exp: Executive Order 13166 ('Improving Access to Services for Persons with Limited English Proficiency') requires all federal agencies to examine their services and improve access for LEP individuals. It also requires federally funded entities (hospitals, courts, schools, social services) to do the same.

interpreter.q: Using a minor (child) as an interpreter in a medical or legal setting is:
choices: Encouraged — children often speak better English than parents
Allowed only if no professional interpreter is available
Strongly discouraged and potentially a rights violation — it places an inappropriate burden on the child and compromises accuracy
Standard practice in NYC schools
interpreter.exp: Professional standards and federal guidance (DHHS, DOJ) strongly discourage using children as interpreters in medical, legal, or social services settings. Children may mistranslate, be traumatized by content, or filter information to protect their parents. Professional interpreters must be provided.

interpreter.q: NYC Administrative Code §8-102(31) defines 'gender' broadly and protects against discrimination. The equivalent statute that covers language access in city-funded programs is:
choices: NYC Administrative Code §20-120
NYC Charter §18
NYC Executive Order 120 (2008), implemented through agency-level Language Access Implementation Plans (LAIPs)
NYC Health Code §505
interpreter.exp: EO 120 requires each city agency with 1,000+ LEP service contacts per year to create a Language Access Implementation Plan (LAIP), publish translated vital documents in the top languages spoken, and designate a Language Access Coordinator. Each agency's LAIP is publicly available.

interpreter.q: Immigration courts in the United States are administered by:
choices: The federal district court system
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS)
The Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR), part of the Department of Justice
The US Supreme Court
interpreter.exp: EOIR (Executive Office for Immigration Review) is the agency within the Department of Justice that manages the immigration court system. Immigration judges are DOJ employees — not Article III federal judges. Immigration court proceedings are administrative, not criminal, which affects the rights afforded to respondents.

interpreter.q: What is a Notice to Appear (NTA) in immigration proceedings?
choices: A letter inviting an immigrant to apply for citizenship
A document charging a person with a crime
The document that initiates removal proceedings by ordering a non-citizen to appear before an immigration judge
A work permit
interpreter.exp: The NTA (Form I-862) is the charging document in immigration court. It identifies the non-citizen, states the charges (e.g., overstaying a visa), and orders them to appear before an immigration judge. Receiving an NTA initiates removal (deportation) proceedings.

interpreter.q: What is ASYLUM?
choices: A type of work visa for agricultural workers
Protection granted to someone who can prove they face persecution in their home country based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group
A legal status for people who have lived in the US for over 10 years
A temporary visa while a green card application is pending
interpreter.exp: Asylum is a form of protection under the Refugee Act (1980) and international law. To be granted asylum, a person must show a well-founded fear of persecution based on one of five protected grounds. People may apply affirmatively (with USCIS) or defensively (in immigration court during removal proceedings).

interpreter.q: 'Removal proceedings' in immigration court means:
choices: A request for a new trial
The government process to deport a non-citizen from the United States
A motion to remove evidence from a case
The process of moving a case from state to federal court
interpreter.exp: Removal (formerly called 'deportation') proceedings are administrative hearings before an immigration judge where DHS (through ICE) seeks to remove a non-citizen. The respondent has the right to be represented by an attorney (at their own expense) and to present evidence and witnesses.

interpreter.q: What is 'voluntary departure' in immigration court?
choices: Being deported forcibly by ICE
Agreeing to leave the US by a set date, which avoids a formal order of removal and its legal bars to future entry
Voluntarily applying for a green card
Leaving the US permanently without any court proceedings
interpreter.exp: Voluntary departure allows a respondent to leave the US voluntarily within a set time period (up to 120 days), avoiding a formal removal order. A formal order of removal can trigger bars to re-entry (3 or 10 years, or permanent). Voluntary departure may preserve options for future immigration benefits.

interpreter.q: What is the Board of Immigration Appeals (BIA)?
choices: The first-level immigration court that hears all removal cases
The appellate body within EOIR that reviews decisions of immigration judges
A court for asylum seekers only
The federal appeals court for immigration matters
interpreter.exp: The BIA is the highest administrative immigration court within EOIR. It reviews decisions made by immigration judges on appeal. BIA decisions can be further appealed to the federal Circuit Courts of Appeals. Interpreters may be needed at BIA hearings, which can be conducted in person or by video.

interpreter.q: What is 'withholding of removal'?
choices: A temporary stay of deportation granted automatically to all asylum seekers
A form of protection that prevents removal to a country where a person's life or freedom would be threatened — a higher standard than asylum
The same as asylum
A bond that prevents someone from being removed while a case is pending
interpreter.exp: Withholding of removal under INA §241(b)(3) requires showing a 'clear probability' of persecution — a higher standard than the 'well-founded fear' required for asylum. Unlike asylum, it does not lead to a green card and has no time limit for filing. It is not available to those convicted of particularly serious crimes.

interpreter.q: Under CAT (Convention Against Torture), a person may not be removed to a country where:
choices: They cannot speak the official language
They would face economic hardship
They would be tortured by or with the acquiescence of the government — regardless of any criminal conviction
They have no family members
interpreter.exp: The Convention Against Torture (implemented in US law through the Foreign Affairs Reform and Restructuring Act) prohibits return to a country where someone would more likely than not be tortured by or with the acquiescence of a government official. Unlike asylum, CAT protection cannot be denied based on criminal history.

dmv.q: What shape is a STOP sign?
choices: Triangle
Square
Octagon (8 sides)
Diamond
dmv.exp: A STOP sign is an octagon (8-sided shape) with a red background and white letters.

dmv.q: What does a YIELD sign mean?
choices: Stop completely before entering the intersection
Slow down and give the right-of-way to traffic that is close enough to be a hazard
Maintain your speed — you have the right-of-way
Honk to warn other drivers
dmv.exp: A YIELD sign means slow down, prepare to stop if necessary, and give right-of-way to vehicles or pedestrians that are close enough to be a hazard.

dmv.q: At a STEADY RED traffic light, what must you do?
choices: Slow down and proceed if the intersection is clear
Stop — do not go until the light turns green
Yield to oncoming traffic then proceed
Proceed only if no pedestrians are in the crosswalk
dmv.exp: A steady red light means STOP. You must not go until the light turns green. You may make a right turn on red only after coming to a full stop and yielding to traffic and pedestrians.

dmv.q: What does a STEADY YELLOW traffic light mean?
choices: Speed up to get through the intersection before red
The light is about to turn green
Slow down — the light is about to turn red
Pedestrians have the right-of-way
dmv.exp: A steady yellow light warns you that the signal is about to turn red. You should slow down and prepare to stop if it is safe to do so.

dmv.q: What color and shape are WARNING signs (like curve ahead, school zone)?
choices: Red octagon
Yellow diamond with black letters or symbols
Green rectangle
Blue square
dmv.exp: Warning signs are YELLOW with black letters or symbols. They are usually diamond-shaped and warn of hazardous conditions or special rules ahead.

dmv.q: What is the standard speed limit in a SCHOOL ZONE in New York State?
choices: 10 mph
15 mph
20 mph
25 mph
dmv.exp: The speed limit in a school zone is 15 mph when children are present. School zone signs mark the reduced speed area.

dmv.q: What is the maximum speed limit on most New York State expressways?
choices: 45 mph
55 mph
65 mph
75 mph
dmv.exp: The speed limit on most expressways is normally 55 mph, unless posted otherwise. Some highways may allow up to 65 mph.

dmv.q: In New York State, who is required to wear a seat belt?
choices: Only the driver
